full_response:
Let m be the number of bits in the table A and let  

h₁ ,h₂ ,…,h_k :  W  ⟶ {1,…,m}  

be k independent, perfectly uniform hash functions.  
Throughout this part we assume  

• m = 20 n ,  
• k is the value obtained in part (b) (k = ⌈(m/n) ln 2⌉ ≈ 20 ln 2 ≈ 14 , a constant),  
• for every 1 ≤ j ≤ m  

  Pr[ A[j] = 1 ] = 1/2.                                            (∗)

(∗) is the standard Bloom–filter estimate (after n insertions each bit
is set with probability (1-1/m)^{kn}=1/2), but it is given to us as an
assumption.  The bits are not independent; we therefore treat
dependence explicitly.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  A collision among the k hash values of one word is unlikely
--------------------------------------------------------------------

For the fixed word w let

    C  :=  { ∃ i ≠ j  with  h_i(w)=h_j(w) }.

For every ordered pair (i ,j) with i<j set  

    X_{ij} = 1  iff  h_i(w)=h_j(w).

Because the hash functions are independent and uniform,

    Pr[X_{ij}=1] = 1/m.

There are  ( k choose 2 ) = k(k–1)/2  such pairs, hence by the union
bound

        Pr[C]
    =   Pr[ ⋁_{i<j}  X_{ij}=1 ]
    ≤   ∑_{i<j} Pr[X_{ij}=1 ]
    =    k(k-1) / (2m)
    <    k² / (2m).                                             (1)

With m = 20 n and the constant k of part (b) this becomes

        Pr[C] ≤ k² /(40 n) = Θ(1/n).                            (2)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Probability that all queried bits are 1 when no collision occurs
--------------------------------------------------------------------

Let  F  be the event that LOOKUP(w) returns TRUE although w∉W, i.e.

    F := { A[h₁(w)]=…=A[h_k(w)]=1 }.

Write F = F∧C  ∨  F∧¬C and use the law of total probability:

    Pr[F] = Pr[F∧C] + Pr[F∧¬C]
          ≤ Pr[C]   + Pr[F | ¬C].                                (3)

We have already bounded Pr[C].  It remains to bound
Pr[F | ¬C], the chance that every inspected bit is 1 when the k
indices are distinct.

Condition on ¬C.  
Because the k indices are now guaranteed to be different and each of
the m positions is equally likely, symmetry together with (∗) implies

    Pr[ A[h_i(w)] = 1 | ¬C ] = 1/2    for every 1≤i≤k.

For two different indices j ≠ ℓ, the events
“A[j]=1’’ and “A[ℓ]=1’’ are not independent, but for the Bloom-filter
process the dependence is of order 1/m.
A standard balls-into-bins (Poisson) calculation shows that

    | Pr[ A[j]=1 ∧ A[ℓ]=1 ] – (1/2)² | ≤ O(1/m).

Extending the calculation to any fixed set of k distinct positions
gives

    Pr[ all k of them are 1 ] = (1/2)^k  ±  O(k²/m)
                               = (1/2)^k  ±  O(1/n).             (4)

In particular,
             Pr[F | ¬C]  ≤  (1/2)^k + O(1/n).                    (5)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Putting everything together
--------------------------------------------------------------------

Insert (2) and (5) into (3):

    Pr[ LOOKUP(w) fails ]                        (= Pr[F])
    ≤   (1/2)^k  +  O(1/n)  +  O(1/n)
    =    (1/2)^k  +  O(1/n).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Result
--------------------------------------------------------------------

We have shown

    Pr[ ∃ i≠j  :  h_i(w)=h_j(w) ]   <   k² /(2m),

and, with m = 20 n and k from part (b),

    Pr[ LOOKUP(w) fails ]  ≤  (1/2)^k  +  O(1/n).

Both statements were required.

\boxed{ \Pr[\text{collision among }h_1,\dots ,h_k] < k^{2}/(2m),\qquad
        \Pr[\text{LOOKUP}(w)\text{ fails}] \le (1/2)^{k}+O(1/n) }  
Confidence: 0.29
